steps.title: Recognize that sample results are estimates, not guarantees
steps.content:
The survey found 78% satisfaction among 1,000 randomly selected adults from one city.

A critical principle: a sample statistic estimates the population parameter but does not prove it. Different random samples from the same population will have different sample proportions due to sampling variability.

steps.title: Evaluate why none of the three statements must be true
steps.content:
Statement I claims the population must be 78% satisfied — but the sample is just an estimate; the true population proportion could be 74%, 76%, 79%, or another nearby value.

Statement II assumes another random sample must also yield exactly 78% — but sampling variability means different samples produce different results (we might observe 75%, 80%, etc. instead).

Statement III claims the result must apply to a different city — but different populations have different characteristics, and we have no information about satisfaction levels elsewhere. None of these statements must be true.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Since none of the three statements logically follows from this single sample survey, that matches choice A — A is correct.
